summary: Grandson of Genghis Khan (son of his youngest son, Tolui) who won the 1260-1264 Toluid Civil War against his younger brother Ariq Boke and founded China's Yuan dynasty as its first emperor. Completed the decades-long Mongol conquest of China by finally absorbing the Southern Song in 1279. Divided his subjects into a four-tier caste system topped by Mongols and a foreigner "semu" caste, and commissioned the Phags-pa script. Held court at Shangdu ("Xanadu" to Europeans) and Khanbaliq, where he was visited by the Venetian traveler Marco Polo. Launched failed invasions of Japan (twice destroyed by "divine wind" typhoons), Vietnam, Java, and Burma's Pagan Kingdom.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Kublai Khan is identified in giveaways: grandson of Genghis Khan (son of Tolui, Genghis's youngest son) and founder/first emperor of the Yuan dynasty. His court at Shangdu, mythologized by Europeans as "Xanadu," and his hosting of the Venetian traveler Marco Polo are so central to his identity that they double as giveaways in nearly every question.

clues.clue: First emperor of the Yuan dynasty, a grandson of Genghis Khan and son of Genghis's youngest son, Tolui
examples: name this first ruler of the Yuan dynasty, a grandson of Genghis Khan who was visited by Marco Polo.
This grandson of Genghis founded the Yuan Dynasty.

clues.clue: Held court at Shangdu, a garden palace Europeans called "Xanadu," visited by the Venetian traveler Marco Polo
examples: Rustichello da Pisa was dictated a narrative of the empire of this ruler, whose garden at Shangdu was called Xanadu by Europeans.
His court at Shangdu was visited by Marco Polo.

works.name: The Toluid Civil War (1260-1264)
works.description: After their brother Mongke Khan died suddenly without an heir, Kublai fought a four-year civil war against their younger brother Ariq Boke for control of the Mongol Empire. Kublai won, but at the cost of the empire's unity — the western khanates never fully accepted his authority again.

clues.clue: Fought and defeated his younger brother Ariq Boke for the Mongol throne, in a war triggered by the sudden, heirless death of their brother Mongke Khan (1260-1264)
examples: This 1260 to 1264 war was precipitated by the sudden death of Mongke Khan without heirs.
name this civil war between the first Yuan Emperor and his youngest brother, a conflict often named for their father.

clues.clue: At the war's outset, the two brothers declared rival kurultais — his own at Kaiping, Ariq Boke's at Karakorum
examples: At the outset of this conflict, the two sides declared rival kurultais in Kaiping and Karakorum.
The kurultai at Kaiping chose this man, who won the Toluid Civil War but lost control of most of the Mongol Empire.

clues.clue: After winning, ordered the commander Liu Bingzhong to build a new capital at Yanjing
examples: At the conclusion of this conflict, the winning commander ordered Liu Bingzhong to build a new capital at Yanjing.

clues.clue: Styled himself "Zhongtong" ("the moderate ruler") and promised tax cuts to win local Chinese support during the war
examples: In order to garner support from the local populace, the winner in this conflict styled himself as Zhongtong, or the "moderate ruler," and promised to reduce taxes.

clues.clue: Ariq Boke's ally Alghu betrayed him after Ariq Boke lost a battle at Shimultai, slaughtering the tax collectors Ariq Boke had sent to his camp
examples: After losing a battle at Shimultai, the losing side was betrayed by their ally Alghu, who slaughtered the tax collectors sent to his camp.

clues.clue: The western Mongol khanates were kept from intervening on Ariq Boke's behalf by a separate war between Berke and Hulagu
examples: The western khanates were prevented from interfering in it by the war between Berke and Hulagu.

clues.clue: After winning, became bogged down in a decades-long war with his nephew Kaidu
examples: Subsequent to this conflict, the winner was bogged down in a decades-long war with his nephew Kaidu.

works.name: The Mongol Conquest of China (completed 1279)
works.description: Kublai completed the multi-generation Mongol campaign to conquer China, which had already absorbed the Western Xia and Jin dynasties, by finally subjugating the Southern Song. The general Bayan led the decisive push, culminating in the destruction of the last Song loyalist fleet at the Battle of Yaishan (also called the Battle of Yamen) in 1279.

clues.clue: Completed the Mongol conquest of China, absorbing the Southern Song after the earlier conquests of Xi Xia (Western Xia) and the Jin dynasty
examples: name this initiative under which Xi Xia, Jin, and finally the Song lands themselves were all subsumed to the rule of Kublai Khan.

clues.clue: The campaign's final push was completed by his general Bayan; at the 1273 Battle of Xiangyang, the Southern Song general Lu Wenhuan surrendered to Kublai
examples: This campaign was completed by the general Bayan, and it caused the one-day rule of Modi following the suicide of Aizong.
At the 1273 Battle of Xiangyang, the Southern Song under Lu Wenhuan surrendered to this man.

clues.clue: An attempt to form an alliance against the campaign used the warning "when the lips are gone, the teeth will feel the cold"
examples: An attempt to form an alliance against this campaign was made with the warning "when the lips are gone, the teeth will feel the cold."

clues.clue: The advisor Yelu Chucai urged mercy during the campaign and was appointed head of the Central Secretariat
examples: During this campaign, Yelu Chucai advised mercy and was appointed head of the Central Secretariat.

clues.clue: The naval Battle of Jiaoshan occurred late in the campaign, shortly before the regent Xie took power following the death of the Song emperor Duzong
examples: The naval battle of Jiaoshan occurred during this campaign, shortly before the regent Xie took power following the death of Duzong.

clues.clue: The campaign was concluded in 1279 at the naval Battle of Yaishan/Yamen, where his fleet, though outnumbered ten-to-one off Guangdong, crushed the last Song resistance
examples: The Battle of Yaishan successfully concluded this campaign in 1279.
whose naval forces were outnumbered ten to one off the coast of Guangdong but still managed to win the Battle of Yamen.

works.name: Foreign Campaigns & Invasions
works.description: Beyond China, Kublai pursued an ambitious and frequently unsuccessful program of foreign conquest — twice-failed invasions of Japan, a naval defeat in Vietnam, a repulsed expedition to Java, and a more successful conquest of Burma's Pagan Kingdom.

clues.clue: Ordered two failed invasions of Japan; both fleets were destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called "divine wind" (kamikaze)
examples: His fleets were twice destroyed by typhoons referred to as "divine wind."
This ruler's attempted invasions of Japan resulted in the destruction of his fleet twice by typhoons, or kamikaze.

clues.clue: After annexing the Goryeo Kingdom (Korea), used Korean shipbuilders to prepare the invasion fleet, provoked partly by the shogunate regent Hojo Tokimune's defiance
examples: After annexing the Goryeo Kingdom, this ruler used Korean shipbuilders to prepare for an invasion spurred by Hojo Tokimune's impudence.

clues.clue: To overcome fear of him before the invasion, a Japanese leader consulted his religious adviser Mugaku Sogen, who told him to scream "victory" until his fear subsided
examples: To overcome a fear of this ruler, a leader consulted his religious adviser, Mugaku Sogen, who urged the leader to scream "victory" until his fear subsided.

clues.clue: His fleet was defeated at the Battle of Bach Dang by the Tran dynasty; the Tran nonetheless later joined the Champa kingdoms in accepting his supremacy over Vietnam
examples: This ruler's fleet was defeated at the Battle of Bach Dang by the Tran dynasty, which later joined with the Champa kingdoms in accepting this man's supremacy over Vietnam.

clues.clue: Sent a punitive naval expedition to Java that was repulsed after Raden Wijaya double-crossed the Mongol forces, indirectly enabling the founding of the Majapahit Empire in 1293
examples: Raden Wijaya laid the foundations of the Majapahit in 1293 by double-crossing and repulsing an expedition to Java sent by this Mongol ruler.

clues.clue: His conquest of Burma's Pagan Kingdom caused its collapse, leaving the Shan peoples greater regional autonomy
examples: The Shan peoples gained much autonomy after the conquest of Pagan by this leader.
The Pagan kingdom's demise was brought about by an expedition ordered by this Mongol ruler.

clues.clue: Handscrolls illustrating his armies in battle depict "thunder crash bombs," an early form of explosive shell launched from catapults
examples: A set of handscrolls illustrating this ruler's armies in battle shows the use of "thunder crash bombs," an early form of explosive shell launched from catapults.

works.name: Governance & Culture
works.description: As emperor, Kublai organized his multiethnic realm through a rigid caste hierarchy and new institutions of indirect rule, while also standardizing a new script for the empire's many languages — though his court was not without its scandals.

clues.clue: Divided his subjects into a four-tier caste system, with a "semu" caste of foreigners ranked above Han Chinese
examples: This ruler divided his people into four castes, one of which consisted of foreigners called semu.
whose four-class caste system placed Han Chinese people below Mongols.

clues.clue: Commissioned the creation of the Phags-pa script
examples: The creation of the Phags-pa script was commissioned by this ruler.

clues.clue: Converted Yunnan's conquered Dali Kingdom into a "tusi" chiefdom, a system of hereditary indirect rule over ethnic minorities
examples: Yunnan's Dali Kingdom was converted into a tusi chiefdom by this first Yuan emperor.

clues.clue: After learning that his minister Ahmad Fanakati kept human skins in his closet, ordered the man's corpse thrown to dogs
examples: After this ruler was told that a minister kept human skins in his closet, he ordered the corpse of that man, Ahmad Fanakati, thrown to dogs.

works.name: Legacy: Marco Polo & Later Depictions
works.description: Kublai's court was immortalized (and embellished) by the Venetian traveler Marco Polo's account, dictated to a fellow prisoner while jailed in Genoa, which centuries later inspired a modernist novel imagining their conversations.

clues.clue: His guest Marco Polo's account of his empire was dictated to Rustichello da Pisa, a fellow prisoner, while the two were jailed together in Genoa
examples: While they were imprisoned together in Genoa, this Pisan author jazzed up Marco Polo's story with language plucked from his own Arthurian romance.
Rustichello da Pisa was dictated a narrative of the empire of this ruler.

clues.clue: The embellished Marco Polo stories inspired Italo Calvino's novel Invisible Cities, framed as a dialogue between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan
examples: The embellishments made to Marco Polo's encounters with Kublai Khan inspired this author's novel Invisible Cities, framed as a dialogue between the two.

comprehensive_summary: Kublai Khan (born 1215) was the son of Genghis Khan's youngest son, Tolui, and grandson of Genghis Khan himself. After the sudden, heirless death of their brother Mongke Khan, Kublai fought a four-year civil war (1260-1264), the Toluid Civil War, against their younger brother Ariq Boke; the two sides declared rival kurultais, Kublai's at Kaiping and Ariq Boke's at Karakorum, while the western Mongol khanates were kept from intervening by a separate war between Berke and Hulagu. Kublai styled himself "Zhongtong" ("the moderate ruler") and promised tax cuts to win local Chinese support, and after Ariq Boke's ally Alghu betrayed him following a defeat at Shimultai, Kublai prevailed — ordering the commander Liu Bingzhong to build a new capital at Yanjing — though he was subsequently bogged down in a decades-long war with his nephew Kaidu and never regained control of the empire's western reaches. Kublai completed the multi-generation Mongol conquest of China, which had already absorbed the Western Xia and Jin dynasties, by finally subjugating the Southern Song; his general Bayan led the decisive campaign, during which the advisor Yelu Chucai urged mercy and was made head of the Central Secretariat, the Southern Song general Lu Wenhuan surrendered after the 1273 Battle of Xiangyang, and the naval Battle of Jiaoshan preceded the regent Xie's rise following the death of the Song emperor Duzong. The campaign concluded in 1279 at the naval Battle of Yaishan (Yamen), where Kublai's fleet, though outnumbered ten-to-one off Guangdong, crushed the last Song resistance. As the first emperor of the Yuan dynasty he founded, Kublai held court at Shangdu, a garden palace Europeans later called "Xanadu," and at Khanbaliq, hosting the Venetian traveler Marco Polo, whose account of the empire was later dictated to a fellow prisoner, Rustichello da Pisa, while the two were jailed together in Genoa; centuries later, the embellished stories of Marco Polo's encounters with Kublai inspired Italo Calvino's novel Invisible Cities. Domestically, Kublai organized his subjects into a four-tier caste system with a "semu" caste of favored foreigners ranked above Han Chinese, commissioned the Phags-pa script, converted the conquered Dali Kingdom of Yunnan into a "tusi" chiefdom of hereditary indirect rule, and, after learning that his minister Ahmad Fanakati had kept human skins in his closet, had the man's corpse thrown to dogs. Beyond China, Kublai pursued an ambitious and frequently unsuccessful program of foreign conquest: he ordered two failed invasions of Japan, both fleets destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called "divine wind" (kamikaze), after annexing the Goryeo Kingdom of Korea and using Korean shipbuilders to prepare his invasion fleet, provoked partly by the shogunate regent Hojo Tokimune's defiance (a Japanese leader reportedly overcame his fear of Kublai only by following his religious adviser Mugaku Sogen's advice to scream "victory"); his fleet was also defeated at the Battle of Bach Dang by Vietnam's Tran dynasty, though the Tran and the Champa kingdoms later accepted his supremacy; a punitive naval expedition to Java was repulsed after Raden Wijaya double-crossed the Mongol forces, indirectly enabling the founding of the Majapahit Empire in 1293; and his conquest of Burma's Pagan Kingdom caused its collapse, leaving the Shan peoples greater regional autonomy.
recursive_suggestions: Mongols — the broader empire topic already exists on this site (output/mongols); this page focuses on Kublai Khan specifically and should stay distinct rather than duplicating that page's Yuan Dynasty / Mongol Invasions of Japan / Mongol Conquest of China sections
Ariq Boke — Kublai's younger brother and rival in the Toluid Civil War; recurring but not yet independently answerlined enough for his own page
Marco Polo — recurring standalone answerline (pairs with Kublai in a joint bonus part); distinct Literature/History topic covering his travels and The Travels of Marco Polo
Majapahit Empire — independently answerlined Southeast Asian topic (Gajah Mada, Hayam Wuruk, Demak Sultanate) that only tangentially involves Kublai's failed Java expedition
Pagan Kingdom — independently answerlined Burmese topic (Anawrahta Minsaw, Shin Arahan, Shwedagon Pagoda) whose collapse is linked to, but distinct from, Kublai's invasion

White Lotus Society / Red Turban Rebellion — excluded here since their bonus part's actual answer was the White Lotus Society and concerns the Yuan dynasty's eventual overthrow, a century after Kublai's death
Song Dynasty — excluded here (Sima Guang, Wang Anshi, Taizu bonus part) since its actual answer was the Song Dynasty itself, not Kublai's conquest of it
